questions.question: Define fractals and explain the concept of self-similarity.
questions.answer: Fractals are geometric figures that display self-similarity, meaning the same pattern appears repeatedly at smaller scales.

questions.question: Explain how the Sierpinski Carpet is constructed.
questions.answer: A square is divided into 9 smaller squares and the central square is removed. This process is repeated for the remaining squares.

questions.question: Explain the construction of the Sierpinski Triangle.
questions.answer: An equilateral triangle is divided into four smaller triangles by joining the midpoints of its sides and removing the central triangle.

questions.question: Describe the steps involved in constructing the Koch Snowflake.
questions.answer: Start with an equilateral triangle, divide each side into three equal parts and construct a triangular bump on the middle segment.

questions.question: Explain why fractals are important in mathematics and nature.
questions.answer: Fractals describe complex natural patterns and help model structures like clouds, mountains and plants.

questions.question: Define faces, edges and vertices with an example.
questions.answer: Faces are flat surfaces, edges are line segments joining faces and vertices are points where edges meet.

questions.question: Explain the structure of a prism.
questions.answer: A prism has two identical polygon bases connected by parallelogram faces.

questions.question: Explain the structure of a pyramid.
questions.answer: A pyramid has a polygon base and triangular faces meeting at a single vertex.

questions.question: What is a net of a solid? Give an example.
questions.answer: A net is a flat pattern that can be folded to form a three-dimensional solid.

questions.question: Explain how to construct a cube using a net.
questions.answer: Six connected squares are drawn and folded along edges to form a cube.

questions.question: Explain the concept of projection.
questions.answer: Projection is the image of a three-dimensional object on a two-dimensional plane.

questions.question: Explain front, top and side views of an object.
questions.answer: Front view shows the object from the front, top view from above and side view from the side.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between shadows and projections.
questions.answer: A shadow formed by light falling on an object represents a projection of that object onto a surface.

questions.question: Explain why projections of parallel lines remain parallel.
questions.answer: Projection preserves the direction of lines, so parallel lines remain parallel after projection.

questions.question: Explain the shortest path concept on a cube surface.
questions.answer: By unfolding the cube into a net, the shortest surface path becomes a straight line on the net.

questions.question: Explain how projections help represent solids in engineering drawings.
questions.answer: Engineers use front, top and side views to represent objects accurately.

questions.question: Define isometric projection.
questions.answer: Isometric projection is a method of representing a three-dimensional object on a two-dimensional plane using equal angles between axes.

questions.question: Explain the purpose of isometric grids.
questions.answer: Isometric grids help draw three-dimensional figures accurately with equal spacing along three axes.

questions.question: Explain why cube stacking problems help understand projections.
questions.answer: Observing stacked cubes from different directions helps understand how projections represent three-dimensional structures.

questions.question: Explain how shadows change when the light source moves farther away.
questions.answer: As the light source moves farther away, the shadow becomes closer to the object's projection.

questions.question: Explain why projections are useful in geometry.
questions.answer: Projections help understand shapes and spatial relationships of objects.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between nets and solids.
questions.answer: A net is a two-dimensional layout that folds to form a three-dimensional solid.

questions.question: Explain how fractals demonstrate repeating patterns.
questions.answer: Fractals repeat the same pattern at smaller scales, creating complex structures.

questions.question: Explain how engineers use projections to design objects.
questions.answer: Engineers use projections to communicate the dimensions and shape of objects accurately.

questions.question: Explain how isometric drawing helps visualize three-dimensional structures.
questions.answer: Isometric drawing provides a clear representation of three-dimensional objects on a flat surface.
